content.text: Vertex AI Agent Builder is Google Cloud's platform for building and deploying AI agents. Its core compute primitive is the
— a managed, serverless environment that hosts your agent code (LangChain, LlamaIndex, or custom) and calls Gemini models through Vertex AI. The service handles scaling, model access, and optional
(RAG via Vertex AI Search or Google Search) without you managing VMs or containers.
a serverless deployment unit that wraps your agent framework code. You implement
(per-request entry point), then deploy to GCP.
built-in tools that Gemini can call natively — Code Execution, YouTube Data, Maps, Vertex AI Search. No Lambda/function needed.
connects Gemini responses to a data source (your Vertex AI Search data store, or the public web via Google Search) to reduce hallucinations with cited source attribution.
the RAG backend — you ingest documents into Vertex AI Search; the agent retrieves relevant chunks before generating
Reasoning Engine supports any Python framework — LangChain, LlamaIndex, or plain Python. You choose the framework; GCP hosts it.

pip install google-cloud-aiplatform[agent_engines,langchain]
# Also install your chosen framework
pip install langchain langchain-google-vertexai
for local development. In GCP environments (Cloud Run, GKE), Application Default Credentials pick up the service account automatically.
The typical pattern: define tools as Python functions, build a LangChain agent executor, then wrap it in a class that extends

import vertexai
from vertexai.preview import reasoning_engines
from langchain_google_vertexai import ChatVertexAI
from langchain.agents import AgentExecutor, create_tool_calling_agent
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.tools import tool

vertexai.init(project="my-gcp-project", location="us-central1")

# ── Define tools ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
@tool
def get_exchange_rate(currency_from: str, currency_to: str) -> str:
    """Get the current exchange rate between two currencies."""
    # Real implementation: call a currency API
    rates = {"USD_EUR": 0.92, "EUR_USD": 1.09}
    key = f"{currency_from}_{currency_to}"
    rate = rates.get(key, "unknown")
    return f"1 {currency_from} = {rate} {currency_to}"

@tool
def get_current_date() -> str:
    """Return the current date in ISO format."""
    from datetime import date
    return date.today().isoformat()

tools = [get_exchange_rate, get_current_date]

# ── Build a LangChain agent ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
llm = ChatVertexAI(model="gemini-1.5-pro", temperature=0)

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a helpful currency assistant. Use tools to answer accurately."),
    ("placeholder", "{chat_history}"),
    ("human", "{input}"),
    ("placeholder", "{agent_scratchpad}"),
])

agent = create_tool_calling_agent(llm, tools, prompt)
agent_executor = AgentExecutor(agent=agent, tools=tools, verbose=True)

# ── Wrap in a Reasoning Engine app class ─────────────────────────────────────
class CurrencyAgent(reasoning_engines.Queryable):
    def set_up(self):
        """Called once when the engine starts. Initialise heavy resources here."""
        self.agent = agent_executor

    def query(self, *, input: str, chat_history: list | None = None) -> str:
        result = self.agent.invoke({
            "input": input,
            "chat_history": chat_history or [],
        })
        return result["output"]
method runs once when the Reasoning Engine container starts. Put expensive initialisation (model loading, connection pooling) here rather than in
Deploy the app class to a managed serverless environment with
. Vertex AI packages your code and dependencies into a container and registers the endpoint.

# Deploy to Vertex AI Reasoning Engine (managed serverless)
from vertexai.preview import reasoning_engines

app = reasoning_engines.ReasoningEngine.create(
    CurrencyAgent(),
    requirements=["google-cloud-aiplatform[agent_engines,langchain]",
                   "langchain", "langchain-google-vertexai"],
    display_name="Currency Agent",
    description="Answers currency conversion questions using live rates",
)

print("Resource name:", app.resource_name)
# Output: projects/123456789/locations/us-central1/reasoningEngines/9876543210
list is pip-installed into the container — keep it minimal for faster cold starts
Deployment takes 2–5 minutes; the resource name is the stable endpoint identifier
You can update an existing engine with
to roll out new agent logic without changing the resource name

# Query the deployed agent
from vertexai.preview import reasoning_engines

# Get a reference to the deployed engine
app = reasoning_engines.ReasoningEngine(
    "projects/123456789/locations/us-central1/reasoningEngines/9876543210"
)

# Single-turn query
response = app.query(input="How many Euros is 150 USD today?")
print(response)

# Multi-turn: maintain chat_history yourself
chat_history = []

turn1 = app.query(input="What is 100 USD in EUR?", chat_history=chat_history)
chat_history.append({"role": "user", "content": "What is 100 USD in EUR?"})
chat_history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": turn1})
print("Turn 1:", turn1)

turn2 = app.query(input="And in GBP?", chat_history=chat_history)
print("Turn 2:", turn2)
Multi-turn conversation history is your responsibility — unlike Azure AI Foundry threads, Reasoning Engine is stateless. Pass
explicitly and maintain it in your application layer.
Grounding with Vertex AI Search and Google Search
Grounding connects model responses to real data, providing source citations and reducing hallucinations. Two modes:

import vertexai
from vertexai.generative_models import GenerativeModel, Tool, grounding

vertexai.init(project="my-gcp-project", location="us-central1")

model = GenerativeModel("gemini-1.5-pro")

# ── Ground responses with Vertex AI Search (your data store) ──────────────────
search_tool = Tool.from_retrieval(
    retrieval=grounding.Retrieval(
        source=grounding.VertexAISearch(
            datastore="projects/my-project/locations/us/collections/default_collection"
                       "/dataStores/my-product-catalog"
        )
    )
)

response = model.generate_content(
    "What colours does the Acme Pro X5 come in?",
    tools=[search_tool],
)
print(response.text)
# The response includes source citations from the data store

# ── Ground with Google Search (public web) ────────────────────────────────────
web_search_tool = Tool.from_google_search_retrieval(
    google_search_retrieval=grounding.GoogleSearchRetrieval()
)

response2 = model.generate_content(
    "What is the current weather in Tokyo?",
    tools=[web_search_tool],
)
print(response2.text)
uses a managed search index built from your documents (PDFs, web pages, BigQuery tables); responses cite specific chunks
connects to the public web; ideal for time-sensitive queries (weather, news, prices)
Grounding can be combined with function tools — the model decides when to retrieve vs. when to call a function
Reasoning Engine is framework-agnostic. Here is the same deployment pattern using LlamaIndex's

# Vertex AI Reasoning Engine also supports LlamaIndex agents
import vertexai
from vertexai.preview.reasoning_engines import ReasoningEngine
from llama_index.core.tools import FunctionTool
from llama_index.core.agent import ReActAgent
from llama_index.llms.vertex import Vertex

vertexai.init(project="my-gcp-project", location="us-central1")

def search_products(query: str) -> str:
    """Search the product catalog for items matching the query."""
    return f"Found: Acme Pro X5 (${'{'}499.99{'}'}), Acme Lite ({'{'}$299.99{'}'})"

def check_inventory(product_id: str) -> str:
    """Check inventory level for a product."""
    return f"{product_id}: 42 units in stock"

class ProductAgent(ReasoningEngine):
    def set_up(self):
        llm = Vertex(model="gemini-1.5-pro", temperature=0)
        tools = [
            FunctionTool.from_defaults(fn=search_products),
            FunctionTool.from_defaults(fn=check_inventory),
        ]
        self.agent = ReActAgent.from_tools(tools, llm=llm, verbose=True)

    def query(self, *, input: str) -> str:
        return str(self.agent.chat(input))

# Deploy
app = ReasoningEngine.create(ProductAgent(), requirements=["llama-index", "llama-index-llms-vertex"])
Reasoning Engine is stateless — sessions must be managed externally. Firestore is a natural fit on GCP:

# Vertex AI Agent Builder includes managed sessions for Dialogflow-style agents.
# For Reasoning Engine agents, manage sessions yourself using Firestore or memorystore.
import json
from google.cloud import firestore

db = firestore.Client()

def save_session(session_id: str, chat_history: list) -> None:
    db.collection("agent_sessions").document(session_id).set(
        {"history": json.dumps(chat_history), "updated_at": firestore.SERVER_TIMESTAMP}
    )

def load_session(session_id: str) -> list:
    doc = db.collection("agent_sessions").document(session_id).get()
    if doc.exists:
        return json.loads(doc.to_dict()["history"])
    return []

# Usage
session_id = "user-123-session-456"
history = load_session(session_id)
response = app.query(input="And what about the Lite model?", chat_history=history)
history.append({"role": "user", "content": "And what about the Lite model?"})
history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response})
save_session(session_id, history)
serverless, real-time, strong consistency — good for active session state
lower latency for high-concurrency agents with short sessions
Gemini 1.5 Pro has a 1M-token context window; large histories are technically possible but increase cost and latency — summarise periodically
Vertex AI Agent Builder vs Bedrock Agents vs Azure AI Foundry
Reasoning Engine locks the dependency versions at deploy time; update the
list and redeploy when upgrading LangChain or LlamaIndex
Reasoning Engine does not persist chat history between
produces an agent with no memory of prior turns
the first request after inactivity can take 5–15 seconds while the container starts — use min-replica configuration for latency-sensitive workloads
grounding reduces but does not eliminate hallucinations; citations tell you the source but not that the model summarised it faithfully — validate critical answers
built-in Extensions (Code Execution, Maps) have their own quotas separate from Gemini inference quotas — check extension-specific limits in GCP Console
runs on every cold start; avoid loading gigabytes of data here — use lazy initialisation or external caches (Firestore, Redis)
For simple agents that don't need LangChain or LlamaIndex, use Gemini's native function calling API directly. The model returns structured
objects when it decides a tool is needed; you execute the function and send the result back in the next turn.

# Gemini's native function calling — use directly without LangChain
import vertexai
from vertexai.generative_models import GenerativeModel, Tool, FunctionDeclaration, Part
import json

vertexai.init(project="my-gcp-project", location="us-central1")

# Declare the function schema
get_product_info = FunctionDeclaration(
    name="get_product_info",
    description="Get information about a product by its ID",
    parameters={
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "product_id": {"type": "string", "description": "The product ID"},
            "include_inventory": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Whether to include stock levels"},
        },
        "required": ["product_id"],
    },
)

product_tool = Tool(function_declarations=[get_product_info])
model = GenerativeModel("gemini-1.5-pro", tools=[product_tool])

# Start a multi-turn chat
chat = model.start_chat()
response = chat.send_message("What is the stock level for product P-9001?")

# Check if Gemini decided to call a function
if response.candidates[0].content.parts[0].function_call:
    fc = response.candidates[0].content.parts[0].function_call
    print(f"Calling: {fc.name} with {dict(fc.args)}")

    # Execute the function
    result = {"product_id": "P-9001", "name": "Acme Pro X5", "stock": 42}

    # Send the function result back
    final_response = chat.send_message(
        Part.from_function_response(name=fc.name, response={"content": result})
    )
    print(final_response.text)
Native function calling has lower overhead than LangChain — no agent executor or prompt template boilerplate
The model can call multiple functions in parallel (parallel function calling) if the task requires independent tool calls
Wrap in a Reasoning Engine for deployment, or call from any Python environment — the Vertex AI SDK works outside GCP too
On Vertex AI, multi-agent systems are built by deploying each specialist as a separate Reasoning Engine and having an orchestrator call them as tools. The orchestrator is itself a Reasoning Engine.

# Multi-agent pattern on Vertex AI: orchestrator + specialist agents
# Each Reasoning Engine is an independent stateless service; the orchestrator
# calls specialists via the Vertex AI Reasoning Engine API.

import vertexai
from vertexai.preview import reasoning_engines
from langchain_google_vertexai import ChatVertexAI
from langchain.agents import AgentExecutor, create_tool_calling_agent
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.tools import tool

vertexai.init(project="my-gcp-project", location="us-central1")

# Reference to a deployed specialist Reasoning Engine
specialist_resource = "projects/123/locations/us-central1/reasoningEngines/9999"
specialist_app = reasoning_engines.ReasoningEngine(specialist_resource)

@tool
def analyse_financial_data(query: str) -> str:
    """Delegate financial analysis to the specialist agent."""
    return specialist_app.query(input=query)

# Orchestrator uses the specialist as a tool
llm = ChatVertexAI(model="gemini-1.5-pro", temperature=0)
prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You route complex queries to specialist agents."),
    ("human", "{input}"),
    ("placeholder", "{agent_scratchpad}"),
])
orchestrator_executor = AgentExecutor(
    agent=create_tool_calling_agent(llm, [analyse_financial_data], prompt),
    tools=[analyse_financial_data],
)

class OrchestratorAgent(reasoning_engines.Queryable):
    def set_up(self):
        self.agent = orchestrator_executor

    def query(self, *, input: str) -> str:
        return self.agent.invoke({"input": input})["output"]
each Reasoning Engine scales independently based on request load
specialists can use different agent frameworks — one LangChain, one LlamaIndex — as long as each exposes a
orchestrator-to-specialist calls cross a network boundary; design tasks so the orchestrator batches requests rather than making many sequential calls
Evaluation with Vertex AI Eval Service
Vertex AI's Eval Service assesses agent response quality using either rule-based metrics or Gemini as a judge. Embed evaluation into your CI/CD pipeline to catch regressions before deployment.

# Vertex AI provides eval utilities for assessing agent response quality.
# The SDK compares agent outputs against golden references or uses Gemini as a judge.

from vertexai.preview.evaluation import EvalTask
from vertexai.generative_models import GenerativeModel

# Define a dataset: inputs and reference outputs
eval_dataset = {
    "prompt": [
        "How many Euros is 100 USD?",
        "What date is it today?",
        "Convert 50 GBP to JPY.",
    ],
    "reference": [
        "100 USD is approximately 92 EUR at current rates.",
        "Today is 2026-07-01.",
        "50 GBP is approximately 9,500 JPY at current rates.",
    ],
}

# Evaluate using a Pointwise metric: Gemini judges each response independently
eval_task = EvalTask(
    dataset=eval_dataset,
    metrics=["summarization_quality", "groundedness", "instruction_following"],
    experiment="currency-agent-eval-v1",
)

# Run the agent on all prompts and evaluate
results = eval_task.evaluate(
    model=GenerativeModel("gemini-1.5-pro"),   # or your deployed agent
    experiment_run_name="run-001",
)
print(results.summary_metrics)
# {"summarization_quality/mean": 0.91, "groundedness/mean": 0.87, ...}
: measures whether the response is supported by retrieved context (important for grounding scenarios)
: measures whether the response follows the system instruction and user request
Results are logged to Vertex AI Experiments for tracking across versions
deploys agent code (any Python framework) to a managed serverless Reasoning Engine; Vertex AI handles scaling and model access
wraps your LangChain, LlamaIndex, or custom agent in a GCP-hosted container via
connects Gemini to Vertex AI Search (your data) or Google Search (public web) for cited, up-to-date responses
provide built-in tools (Code Execution, Maps, YouTube) that Gemini can call without Lambda functions
maintain chat history externally (Firestore, Redis) and pass it on each
distinguishes Vertex AI from Bedrock Agents and Azure AI Foundry — you bring your own agent logic and orchestration patterns
